transcript_facts:
*   The patient reports fatigue for the past two months.
*   The patient denies blood in stool.
*   The clinician suspects cervical radiculopathy.
*   The clinician plans a cervical MRI.
*   The patient reports worsening headaches over the past few months.
*   The patient reports no trouble with headaches in the past.
*   All labs were normal.
*   The MRI reveals a pituitary lesion with elevation and compression of the optic chiasm.
*   The ventricles are normal in size and no other abnormalities are noted.
*   The clinician is Dr. Flores.
*   The patient reports headaches located behind his eyes.
*   The patient describes the pain as a dull nagging ache.
*   The patient reports the onset of headaches was about three months ago.
*   The patient reports the headaches have been getting worse over time.
*   The patient reports the headaches are now about six out of 10 severity.
*   The patient reports the headaches tend to be worse in the morning.
*   The patient reports the headaches last a few hours at a time.
*   The patient reports no pain radiates.
*   The patient denies being sick or feeling sick.
*   The patient denies a fever or rash.
*   The patient denies neck issues.
*   The patient denies numbness or tingling.
*   The patient reports he has never passed out.
*   The patient reports he seems to be bumping into door frames recently.
*   The patient reports no change in his vision.
*   The patient reports his eyes were checked in the fall.
*   The patient reports he has no other medical problems and takes no medicines.
*   The patient tried Tylenol a few times for the headaches but it didn't work, so he stopped.
*   The patient reports he has no family history of diseases.
*   The patient works as a computer programmer.
*   The patient is married and has been married for 25 years.
*   The patient bought a small house.
*   The patient does not drink any alcohol, smoke, or use recreational drugs.
*   The clinician is performing an examination.
*   The patient is alert, oriented to time, place, and person.
*   The patient’s affect is appropriate and speech is fluent.
*   The cranial nerve examination is grossly intact.
*   The visual acuity and eye movements are normal.
*   The pupils are equal and reactive.
*   The visual field testing reveals bitemporal hemianopia.
*   The patient reports color vision is normal.
*   The MRI appears to be a benign pituitary adenoma.
*   The mass is pressing on the optic chiasm.
*   The visual field test will map peripheral vision.
*   The surgeon will perform a transsphenoidal approach.
*   There is a risk of anesthesia including but not limited to the risk of heart attack, stroke, and death.
*   There is a risk of surgery including infection, need for further surgery, wound issues such as spinal fluid leak or infection, seizure, stroke, permanent numbness, weakness, difficulty speaking, or even death.
*   The patient understands the need for surgery.
*   The patient looks forward to these headaches going away.
